region_of_origin: China (Cantonese); widely adopted in Malaysia and Singapore
food_subgroup: Steamed and fried Cantonese small bites; mixed dim sum assortment

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03315873 represents the dim sum food product category; used as closest parent as no single-item exact code exists for a mixed dim sum assortment.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKW covers dim sum as a category; used as closest parent for a mixed assortment record.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for dim sum as a food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical dim sum serving consists of 3 to 4 pieces totalling approximately 120 g, as commonly served in Malaysian and Singaporean Chinese restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Dim Sum (mixed assortment) — nutrient composition estimated from IMR Malaysia MyFCD component entries for har gow, siu mai, and char siu bao, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entries for steamed pork dumplings and shrimp dumplings
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research (IMR) Malaysia — Malaysian Food Composition Database (MyFCD); USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single MyFCD or USDA entry covers a mixed dim sum assortment. Values are estimated as a weighted average of three representative dim sum items: har gow (steamed shrimp dumpling), siu mai (steamed pork and shrimp dumpling), and char siu bao (steamed BBQ pork bun), weighted equally. USDA FoodData Central entries for steamed pork dumplings (FDC ID 168927) and shrimp dumplings were used to cross-validate macro ranges. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of steamed dumplings and dim sum items — analogous estimate based on steamed wheat-flour dumplings (GI ~48) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a mixed Malaysian dim sum assortment. GI of 48 is estimated from the closest analogous food: steamed wheat-flour dumplings with meat filling, which appear in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) with GI values in the range 45–52. A midpoint of 48 is used. Glycemic load per serving = (48 × 23.5 × 1.20) / 100 = 13.5 g, based on 120 g serving size and 23.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g; ~576 mg per standard 120 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit dim sum intake or choose steamed varieties with reduced dipping sauce, as a single serving can contribute approximately 25% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate content with low glycemic index (GI ~48)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dim sum has a low glycemic index (~48) and moderate carbohydrate load, making it a manageable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when portion size is controlled to 3–4 pieces per meal and high-sugar dipping sauces are avoided.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat and high sodium; deep-fried varieties increase fat load
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should prefer steamed dim sum over deep-fried varieties to minimise saturated fat intake, and should limit soy-sauce-based dips to reduce sodium exposure, as the combined fat and sodium profile poses a moderate cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dim sum (點心, Dian Xin) is a style of Cantonese Chinese cuisine consisting of small, bite-sized portions served in steamer baskets or on small plates. Originating in southern China, dim sum has become a staple of Malaysian and Singaporean Chinese food culture, traditionally enjoyed during yum cha (morning or midday tea) sessions. A typical assortment includes har gow (steamed shrimp dumplings), siu mai (open-topped pork and shrimp dumplings), char siu bao (steamed or baked BBQ pork buns), cheung fun (rice noodle rolls), and lo mai gai (glutinous rice in lotus leaf). The textures range from delicate and silky (steamed dumplings) to crispy and golden (deep-fried taro puffs). Flavours are savoury, umami-rich, and subtly seasoned with soy sauce, sesame oil, and ginger. Nutritionally, a mixed dim sum serving provides moderate energy (~195 kcal per 100 g), moderate protein (~9.5 g), and moderate carbohydrate (~23.5 g) with a low glycemic index (~48). Sodium content is notably high (~480 mg per 100 g), primarily from soy sauce and seasoning, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The low GI makes dim sum a relatively manageable carbohydrate source for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in controlled portions without high-sugar sauces.
common_user_queries: How many calories in dim sum?
Is dim sum suitable for diabetics?
What is the sodium content of dim sum?
What are the types of dim sum?
What is the GI of dim sum?

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry covers a mixed Malaysian dim sum assortment. Nutrient values are estimated as a weighted average of three representative dim sum items (har gow, siu mai, char siu bao) from IMR Malaysia MyFCD and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. GI is an analogous estimate from steamed wheat-flour dumplings in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: 1. The input food_name 'Dum Sum' is a phonetic variant/misspelling of 'Dim Sum'; the canonical English name 'Dim Sum' is used in this record. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the IMR MyFCD or USDA SR Legacy entries used for this estimation. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the source databases for this food category. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the source databases for this food category. 5. Halal status is marked false as dim sum commonly contains pork; halal-certified dim sum exists but cannot be assumed for a generic record. 6. The GI of 48 is an analogous estimate based on steamed wheat-flour dumplings with meat filling from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008); gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule. 7. Glycemic load per serving = (48 × 23.5 × 1.20) / 100 = 13.5 g. 8. The input source 'field survey data' is a delivery platform and is a forbidden source for nutrient evidence; it has been used only to preserve food identity metadata (food_id, restaurant name, cuisine tags). 9. The input price fields (0.01 SGD) appear to be placeholder values and are not carried forward into the output record.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Corrected food_name from 'Dum Sum' to canonical 'Dim Sum'. Added full multilingual local_names for Chinese (simplified/traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi. Built comprehensive aliases and aliases_by_language with consistency verification. Estimated nutrient values from IMR MyFCD and USDA SR Legacy weighted average. Set GI=48 from analogous estimate (University of Sydney GI Database). Added health context for all three required conditions. Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d, vitamin_e, selenium) in notes. Verified gi_category='low' per ≤55 threshold. Verified aliases_by_language/flat aliases consistency.
Iteration 2: Reviewed all hard gates and Q criteria; confirmed all pass. No further revisions required.
